So today it is the tenth of May and we are at the African American Museum in Fair Park in Dallas, Texas. This is David Klein talking. David Klein from UNC Southern Oil History Project and Virginia Tech. And I'm here with Charles Chuck Siler. Also with John Bishop who is behind the camera, John Bishop of Media Generation and UCLA. And we're all here today as part of the Civil Rights History Project on behalf of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian's Museum of African American History and Culture. And Chuck, I just wanted to say thank you so much for your part of this project. You're welcome. I'm happy to be here. And if we could start, if you could just tell us a little bit about the family into which you were born. And how you may have seen that environment shaping the kind of work and things that you went on to do. Well, I think what is, I don't have to, I had a big family for one. My mother and father were divorced early though my father and I did get to be good friends later in life. My mother was a beautician. And so naturally she was in a position to hear everybody's business. And I think she was well trusted because she didn't tell her what she heard. My grandmother was a piano player. Her career was spent playing for churches, teaching music, choirs, this and that. And I was her oldest grand, her first grandchild. So I could have been a little bit spoiled. My grandfather, I was the first grandchild that he had, that he had a chance to spend any time with. And he was alive till I was seven. So he was an influence in terms of the fact that he abetted my creativity. I guess my grandmother found me when I was, as she said, about 50, 60 months old. I found a pencil. And we had these lower half of the walls in the house that they had bought were wooden. And she saw me trying to draw on the wall. And her story was that I wasn't just scratching on the wall but I was trying to draw little stick figures and things. So she gave me, told me if I wanted to do that, ask for paper and pencil. And so two of the first words that I learned according to her were paper and pencil because she said I started asking her for that almost every day. But the upside for her was she could put me in a corner with a pen, a big cheap Indian pad, which I remember. And a pencil and leave me there all day. So the upside was she used to always start out reading every day with the cartoons. So the attitude was she'd get a laugh and first they'd get to the serious news. And I come from a family of folk who had, I think, great senses of humor. My father, in fact, also, when I got to know him as an adult, I discovered that his sense of humor was very well intact to him. And my mother is 92 and she still can be funny when she was too. But my mother had six brothers, all of whom had a lot of influence on me. And one of my father's, his oldest brother, Eugene, was another influence. He lived about 40, 50 miles away and laughed for yet, but we were in contact. My family in Baton Rouge was one that encouraged me. All of them were readers. So I grew up reading. I started reading when I was, and my younger brother, when we were both about two. You know, we were able to read a sentence and do things. When I started school, I was five. And they let me, you know, my first grade teacher was a neighbor. And she took me because I was the only black kid in the neighborhood at the time. And so I went to school with her. And I did well enough that she asked the principal who also lived in the neighborhood. But things were different then because my parents, my mother, had gone to school or had been taught by half of the same folk who taught me years later. Everyone knew everyone else. You know, I had relatives who, yeah, I had folk who, one of my aunts was secretary at my high school. Another one taught, taught, taught home economics. It was married to my mother's brother. And so, you know, there were, those people there, my uncle Ralph was head custodian at my high school and became later parish white. Supervisive custodial things in Baton Rouge. So the family was very well known in terms of that. He was also a caterer, great cook, you know. And I learned a lot from that. My wife tells me she married me because I could read and cook. And I have to blame my family for that. But at times too, when I did things that were sort of, as they like to say now out the box, I was supported by that. You guys made me start thinking about this because I was trying to go back to when I first protested something. And my first protest, as much as I laugh about it, now was against Boy Scouts. Because in the 50s, when I was probably 11, 12 years old, we marched in the Boy Scouts parade. And we were proud of the fact that we had little drill teams and we could very moves and this and that. But in Baton Rouge, they put the black boy Scouts behind the horses. And I think I did it the first two years and I had to clean my shoes after that. I decided I wasn't going to do that. I had a scout master who wasn't in line with that with me. So I quit the Boy Scouts and started an explorer post at my school. But it was the first active protest that I did and got away with. I went to McKinley High in Baton Rouge. McKinley was well known for being greater academics, greater athletics, and we would fight anybody and usually win. And South Baton Rouge was, you know, in the city of Irwin, it wasn't as bad as some of the inner cities. Now we didn't shoot as many people. Folk weren't getting a shot, this and that. But you would fight and you had to learn to do that kind of thing. When the Southern University demonstration in March of 1960 happened, they had to lock us on campus. Because we were ready to go out and do it. We started a little bit more about that when you said you were ready to go out. In what way and what had you been? We were ready to go and join the demonstration. You know, we heard about it. Somebody said students from Southern are marching downtown. And most of us were ready to say, let's go and join them. You said you had a sense of what was going on for the Korean and the world community. We knew because we had, you know, I've told kids this and I hate to say, but I think the kids that I used to talk to about the Civil Rights Movement when I worked at the State Museum, you may have that in Louisiana, but there were a few schools that I went to where they were interested in things like the Civil Rights Movement. And there were social studies teachers who had the sides up, the white and black, you know, white and colored at that side. And I told them about things that we used to do to protest. You know, we would take a paper cup, go into the men's room, use it, and pour it over the white water fountain. It was our way of protesting what we could, you know, because we couldn't, you know, and we were treated bad. You know, folk would throw money at you. My uncle Jean started the first black cleaners in Lafayette, Louisiana. He had to, when he, in the early days, sit in his shop at night with his gun. Because they threw bricks, they shot, they said, but he had a reputation for shooting back. And he was, he was a person that, until he died, I love the man. Like, he was, you know, he felt that since my father and mother had divorced and they gone, he felt obligated to be close to me, keep me linked to the silo side of the family well. The silo's had left Alabama, my uncles and my father and that much because they had a reputation for shooting back. And, and mobile, in that area where they were from, in the 30s and 40s, that wasn't a part of it. My father also played baseball, which let him see a lot of it. He had some good friend, Satya Page was one of them that I happened to meet once and I thought that was one of the great moments of my life in 1967. But my father stayed involved with baseball most of his life. He started the Compton County Mac baseball league. And he had, he told me the stories about the fights that they had in California. But that league sent over 600 players to the pros. And when I worked at Southern for a few years later, I started with one of the coaches at Southern League Flint Royate, played ball with my father, put a black pelicans in New Orleans. And when he found out that he played third base, second base and my father was a shortstop. And he said, you're a child or son, dad, I tell you. And I put them in touch and a lot of guys out of Compton went through Southern and to the other HBCUs. So you know, little things like that, that family network of sorts. Most of my uncles, with possibly one exception, worked for themselves. My uncle Peter, who was one of those that I was named after. His name was Ernest, by hits the E. In my name, my uncle TJ was an insurance salesman. Most of them had been in World War II. All of them, my uncle Earl was a tailor. They had learned these things. My two younger uncles had been in Korea. Well, one was in Korea, one was in service as a medic during the Korean War, but he couldn't get a job when he got out. So he worked as a waiter, and like I said, he was the one who later became supervisor in the Baden's Urge School District of custodial services and things. And he had his own business until, long after he retired. The thing was they tended to be independent. My grandfather spoiled me. Like I said, I was the first grandchild that he had in two families to have time with. And so, you know, we would listen to all the old radio shows when he had to babysit me because my grandmother had choir rehearsals and things during the week. This was her living. And she made, for that time, a fairly good living, playing music. Our first thing, and John would appreciate this, that she told me was a story about what got her in the gospel. One part was she was raised by her uncle and aunt, who, her uncle was a Methodist minister named Mansfield Jones. But she got punished because she was playing blues. She liked to play the blues. She played a stried type piano. There's a piano player out there now who I've heard play with her, and I wish I had recorded it. And Henry Butler, who has a style very similar, big left hand, big clapping left hand. And she played the yellow dog blues for me. I have loved the blues ever since. Okay, for all she did with gospel, the blues got me, okay. And a lot of my friends that I grew up with had batted, and we were just blues players and things like that. And so we, you know, are still, I'm still tied to that. The act of civil disobedience, my senior year in high school after the Southern demonstration, a group of us, I'm trying to remember names, Moses Edwards, Betty Jones, E. Nola Price, the oldest Washington who's not with us any longer, were among, we were a part of the end, some of us were in double ACPU Council, folks. Betty Jones. Betty G. Jones, yeah. And I think Moses is still alive. We graduated in class of 60, but after we took our final examinations about two weeks before we were supposed to march, we decided that we were going to pull our protests. And so my family looked up one night and there was with a picket sign in front of the state library, because we had tried to use it. And they didn't let us in. So we, there was a, was there black, there was a black, so called black branch somewhere. There were black, black, black, we had to call the branch library. And I spent a lot of time there because they finally, at one point, moved around the corner from my house. I would, you know, they had to read 12 books and you get the certificate thing. Then I'd read 40 books in the summertime so I could have three or four centimeters, you know. But Miss Bennett, who was the librarian, had a son, Herbert Bennett, who was one of my first teachers, who was a very talented artist. As I said at that time, I had an uncle, Willie Jr., we called him son, who was also a very talented artist. But at that time there was very little outlet there. And my uncle's son had to leave Baton Rouge because he had gotten into a fight with some guys. The best description I can give you of him is he was, he looked like Bert Landcaster in brown face. He was about six feet tall. And he had a Charles Atlas bill, but he had been driving trucks and loading trucks since he was 10, 11 years old for the Belonin brothers at Baton Rouge. And so he had this bill that was there. He died after being shot by another American soldier. Well, he didn't die immediately. He lived nine more years, but he was shot with a machine gun. And he had probably almost 60 surgeries over the years, removing shrapnel as it came to the surface. But during that time, he was very much an influence on me also because he was encouraging when it came to my art. And he showed me that he could draw. And I had never seen anybody do it that well, nobody black. I'd seen all of these other things. And later when I had a chance to study with some, I think, great artist. I was able to appreciate it because some of them had come there. My uncles and my grandmother, particularly when we hit the state library, I'm going back to that, didn't put me out of the house. The part of bad news where I grew up in one point in this history, it's called Markets and Old. We had snakes all over the place. Every kind of snake, you know, I mean my backyard was a veritable. We. To overcome fear. I went and read everything I could all snakes. And one point I wanted to be a herpetologist, I drew snakes, sketch snakes. And I had teachers again like Ben Hall who was my biology teacher. Mr. Hall let me bring a pet snake in for demonstration. It was a hug no snake. Let me doctor a country. His name was Irving. He became a cartoon later too. But Irving was the first opportunity that I had to do this. I had teachers who always taught us, saying when I was in school was that you had to be twice as good if you were black to get half as much. My teacher said, okay, you got to be three, four, five times as good. And we grew up with that. We had silent support from a lot of them even though they couldn't speak up when we went out and protested. Now the reason we did it for the most part after we we had been planning it. But the reason we waited until after we finished our examinations was because of the fact that they couldn't use missing that against us. Okay, and we had about two weeks. We finished examinations and about this time in May, I graduated once the 26th, which I'll never forget because it was my aunt's birthday and later my wedding day. We were married on the 26th of May. But during that time we started our protest. We started picoting, we started doing things. And they had to watch us. And there were other kids at Southern High, a few from Capitol, who were Capitol Avenue, another one of the schools, who were getting involved in the activity because greenville had happened. Then Southern happened. People don't know this, but Southern's first demonstration was the largest of the mass demonstrations to hit the South in 1960. Marvin Robinson, who lives here in Dallas now, he's recovering from a stroke I was told. Donald Moss, who I think is back in Louisiana, I'm not certain. But I've got a friend in Baton Rouge, Mayo, Bruce, who was from Winfield. In Winfield, they tried to integrate the library there and he had a good put to his here about a sheriff. And when he tells that story about it, I've never seen him talk about it without becoming emotional. But if you've ever been shot at him or if you've ever had someone threaten you that way, you don't forget that. And it was something that made me, it made it difficult for me to be nonviolent later. I met Martin Luther King in 1962. I had a chance to shake his hand and he at the NA convention in Atlanta walked around, came around the room and shook every by all the young folks, shook our hands. And gave us a word or two of encouragement, but I'll have to backtrack to get to that point. But they wanted to put us out of school in 60, but they couldn't. One reason being all of the guys that I named that were a part of this that I can remember now were the best students in the class. Our valedictorian and salutatorian didn't participate, but then again, different reasons. Most of us were the ones who had gone through it, who lived out there with it, who put up with it, and we weren't as sheltered. And we were very forward and we were going to be out there saying what we had to say. So we did what we could, we started that. Things settled down a bit and all of us went wherever to school. I went to Southern. You talked a little bit about this already, but the support behind you, I mentioned this again. So clearly you've got some of you have your families. You talked about, I mean, your family so many because of this history of independent business owners and this kind of independence that your family actually had. But you often see in competitions. One of the things that was funny, my uncle Ralph, when my grandmother died and I was talking after the funeral and he told me, he says, you know your grandfather used to write my grandfather wrote a bicycle to work. He had worked on the railroad and as he got two old to work trains, he became the janitor at the roundhouse in Baton Rouge. I remember going with him when I was about three, four years old. He suddenly took me down to Cedar and he's giant trains. You know, when you, that tall in the train is 20 feet, it looks huge and everybody knew Willie, you know, as he was. And he always wrote a bicycle to work. And my uncle Ralph told me, he says, you know, daddy wrote a bike and that's what we all called him daddy. He says to work because he wasn't going to pay a dime and have somebody to tell him where to sit on the bus. Okay, he generally wrote, or cover alls to work. But every Sunday morning he was had Deacon at his church, he was in a suit and he would walk to church, you know, so I've gone with him a few times here there. But he was, you know, he was, he was a model for manhood and I was his oldest grandchild. And at that time he was going to, you know, give it. I didn't know that then but whatever was there was a part of what later made me understand that I could speak up. And I had learned from my mother and from my grandmother that right or wrong, if I felt I was right, I was going to say it. Now sometimes I got spanked for it, but my mother learned early on that I was hard-headed enough that if I knew I was right and she learned that she could speak me and I told her the last time I got a spanking I said, well, you can do this, but I'm not going to cry. And so I took the spanking and I looked back at it and my question was, are you finished? I never got another one, but I had to sacrifice that because then I had my mother and my grandmother in each year in stereo talking to me about what I had done wrong, what I did. So I was kept on track. In school, I had some teachers. Mr. Hall was one in particular. I ran into his grandson a few weeks ago here that I hadn't known. Mr. Altamus, who had been in World War II also with my uncles and things, who had what we now call post-traumatic stress disorder. I didn't understand, you know, guys would laugh because they said they could set off a firecrack at Mr. Altamus with diamonds or the death columns. Well, when I got back from Vietnam, I apologized to Mr. Altamus for having been one of those guys who would laugh when somebody did something like that and say, oh, Mr. Altamus, well, I understood why he drove onto the desk. I had been through rocket attacks and things like that. Okay, so those loud noises, I still got to know us from being too close to a howitzer, you know, without protection. And those kind of things, you know, you learn to appreciate later in life. But these were the guys who also were supporting us quietly. They didn't want to lose their jobs. We didn't want them to lose their jobs. But they didn't say, I never heard you wrong. You know, Mr. Hall has two, some daughters who are still alive who are like little sisters of mine. Because Mr. Hall would sit there and tell you what they, you know, and we heard the stories of what people had gone through and they were reinforced. And in the neighborhood, all of that, Freya Anderson, whose father was a member of the end of, was on the board of the NA in Baton Rouge, we were on a panel together at LSU in February of this year. And Dr. Anderson, who was a dentist, was one of the leaders of the Civil Rights Movement in Baton Rouge. In 1961, I started off, I was a foot soldier, I wasn't one of the leaders. Hewitt Gerard Brown, who later became known as H. Rap Brown. I've known him since junior high school. We got to be close in college because we used to hitchhike to school together. We lived in the same part of town. For protection in part two, because it wasn't easy to be out there, and it was always easier to hitchhike with somebody. We would do that from Southern because we didn't have bus service to the school. If you were up there late at night, we had to get home, so it was either walk. There was a part of Baton Rouge called Dixie that we didn't really want to walk through at night. Because anything could happen on scenic highway. We tried to catch people coming off campus going on, and everybody got to know us over a period of time. The reason he had to leave Baton Rouge was because he got two guys jumped him. It was during a time when they could get away with things like beating up on Black Folk. I knew of cases where cops would have Billy Club practice as some folk we used to call it on people. My uncle, as I said, was one of... He was strong enough to take the front of a Volkswagen. When he got drafted, he was working in a war plan, a munitions plan, a something of North and Kansas or somewhere like that as close as I can get to it. And he had changed his name. He added an R instead of Willie Moton Jr. The family name was Moton in Louisiana. He added an R. He was Willie Morton Jr. That was enough in those days to get out of that. But he went into the military. I don't know what prompted it. But he was a person who would fight back. And generally, he was a person whom if you had a fight with, he was going to... I didn't want him to ever hit me. He made me go back and fight a guy who was bigger than me once. And I stayed in the fight more out of fear of him than getting hurt. And I took a good butt weapon for about half an hour before the other guy gave up. Because every time I wanted to stop, I looked and he'd be standing there just watching me. And after it was all over, he told me, he says, I didn't say you had to win the fight. He said it said you had to fight. And once he says, bullies don't want people to fight back. Oh, he says they were. I never had another fight with that guy here because he understood that I was going to... My uncle died not long after that. One of the brothers, Earl, became a minister in Paris, California. And his son, Don, is a bishop now. I don't know how to do these things. I'm not a good church girl, OK? But the family has a history like that. My uncle, Gene used to tell me, he says, I didn't shoot the sheriff, he says, but I almost shot the deputy. And they were looking for him at one point. And the guy who was the deputy that saw where he was hiding was a friend of his. And knew he wasn't guilty of anything. And told the guy he wasn't there. This is the story he told me later. He said, and so he was able to get away and get away from mobile. And he said, no, he had to leave. And so, you know, I had that growing up. I had these guys, these voices in my, and all of them were standing up. My uncle Ralph, the funniest story that I've ever heard. He was a guy walked up to him when he was on his knees, saying his prayers in the barracks when he was in the army and started playing the dozens with him. And when my uncle Ralph got up, beat the guy. I mean, beat him really good, then turned around, got back on his knees and finished saying his prayers. You know, and I always had these guys saying, you know, so when it came down to that, when I was 19 years old and that activity started, I was going to be a part of it. I think it's a part of what made me decide that maybe at 80, I'm going to become a full time stand-up comic or something. We had been arrested once. There was a rally tonight when we got back. We went back out the next day because they dared us to come back and we took the dare. Okay. Or I don't, if I recall correctly, I didn't stay at home that night because they, so I was afraid somebody might try to talk me out of it. But we went back downtown. One of the things I was kind of good at was whenever we had protest, I used to get to do, I had to stick with voices. And when I knew sometime, you know, I didn't tell the guys I was doing this all the time, but I would get on the phone and call down to the police and call in a report that they had some knee-resout there. And that they were over here, they're all over the place. And they would go this way and we'd be over here. And, you know, I had that streak. I, you know, I still have it. I couldn't be different. But we went back down. They came and they put 340 of us plus in a space that was built for about 42 people.
